records.ety: [OE. shroud, shrud, schrud, AS. scrūd a garment, clothing; akin to Icel. skruð the shrouds of a ship, furniture of a church, a kind of stuff, Sw. skrud dress, attire, and E. shred. See Shred, and cf. Shrood.]

defs: That which clothes, covers, conceals, or protects; a garment.
quotes: Swaddled, as new born, in sable shrouds.

defs: Especially, the dress for the dead; a winding sheet.
That which covers or shelters like a shroud.
quotes: Jura answers through her misty shroud.

defs: A covered place used as a retreat or shelter, as a cave or den; also, a vault or crypt.
quotes: The shroud to which he won His fair-eyed oxen.
A vault, or shroud, as under a church.

defs: The branching top of a tree; foliage.
quotes: The Assyrian wad a cedar in Lebanon, with fair branches and with a shadowing shroad.

defs: A set of ropes serving as stays to support the masts. The lower shrouds are secured to the sides of vessels by heavy iron bolts and are passed around the head of the lower masts.
One of the two annular plates at the periphery of a water wheel, which form the sides of the buckets; a shroud plate.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Shrouded; p. pr. & vb. n. Shrouding.]

defs: To cover with a shroud; especially, to inclose in a winding sheet; to dress for the grave.
quotes: The ancient Egyptian mummies were shrouded in a number of folds of linen besmeared with gums.

defs: To cover, as with a shroud; to protect completely; to cover so as to conceal; to hide; to veil.
quotes: One of these trees, with all his young ones, may shroud four hundred horsemen.
Some tempest rise, And blow out all the stars that light the skies, To shroud my shame.
If your stray attendance be yet lodged, Or shroud within these limits.
